intro: Converting TypeScript to C++ transitions an application from a managed web language into an ultra-high-performance system language that features deterministic resource management. Developers make this structural pivot when building complex graphics systems, game engines, or low-latency computational tools requiring zero-cost abstractions. This requires migrating from garbage-collected reference safety to strict ownership controls and explicit object lifecycles.

diffs.from: Structural type definitions containing flexible union interfaces and compile-time type extraction helpers.
diffs.to: Static, nominal type layout supporting generic template metaprogramming, multiple inheritance models, and type deduction via auto.

diffs.from: C-style block syntax integrating arrow parameters, modular imports, and structured object declarations.
diffs.to: Extensive object-oriented design featuring manual operator overloads, namespaces, and template symbols.

diffs.from: Engine-driven automatic memory collection; objects are tracked dynamically on a managed heap.
diffs.to: Deterministic resource management leveraging RAII patterns, stack allocations, and explicit smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr).

diffs.from: Built-in engine event loops processing background micro-tasks and task queues asynchronously.
diffs.to: Multi-threaded capabilities leveraging system primitives like `std::async`, standard thread objects, and low-level condition variables.

diffs.from: Web and platform execution hooks heavily augmented via external open-source module managers.
diffs.to: Rich Standard Template Library (STL) delivering highly optimized vectors, hash maps, algorithms, and file stream tooling.

examples.title: Class Definitions with Resource Management
examples.description: Creating standard class configurations utilizing proper separation constructs.

examples.title: Dynamic Hash Storage Operations
examples.description: Storing key-value pairings within high-performance lookup engines.
examples.fromCode:
const map = new Map<string, number>();
map.set("age", 30);

tips.title: Leverage RAII and Smart Pointers
tips.body: Avoid using raw pointers with manual `new` and `delete` invocations. Use `std::unique_ptr` and `std::shared_ptr` to ensure that heap-allocated resources are automatically freed when their managing object goes out of scope.

tips.title: Prefer Stack Allocations Natively
tips.body: In TypeScript, all objects are implicitly placed on the heap. In C++, instantiating an object without keywords places it directly on the stack, which guarantees high execution speeds and immediate cleanup.

tips.title: Understand Passing Boundaries
tips.body: TypeScript passes objects by reference. C++ copies parameters by value by default unless specified otherwise; use const references (e.g., `const std::string& text`) to pass large structures efficiently without copying overhead.

tips.title: Replace Array Methods with STL Algorithms
tips.body: Instead of using chained array actions like `.map()`, import the `<algorithm>` header and use utilities like `std::transform` alongside anonymous lambda expressions to modify your underlying data sequences.
